title: DROID-SLAM: Deep Visual SLAM for Monocular, Stereo, and RGB-D Cameras

paragraphs.text:
# **DROID-SLAM: Deep Visual SLAM for Monocular, Stereo, and RGB-D Cameras**

**Zachary Teed Jia Deng** Princeton University `{zteed,jiadeng}@princeton.edu`

## **Abstract**

We introduce DROID-SLAM, a new deep learning based SLAM system. DROIDSLAM consists of recurrent iterative updates of camera pose and pixelwise depth through a Dense Bundle Adjustment layer. DROID-SLAM is accurate, achieving large improvements over prior work, and robust, suffering from substantially fewer catastrophic failures. Despite training on monocular video, it can leverage stereo or RGB-D video to achieve improved performance at test time. The URL to our open source code is `https://github.com/princeton-vl/DROID-SLAM` .

## **1 Introduction**

Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) aims to (1) build a map of the environment and (2) localize the agent within the environment. It is a special form of Structure-from-Motion (SfM) focused on accurate tracking of long-term trajectories. It is a critical capability for robotics, especially autonomous vehicles. In this work, we address _visual_ SLAM, where sensor recordings come in the form of images captured from a monocular, stereo, or RGB-D camera.

The SLAM problem has been approached from a number of different angles. Early work was built using probabilistic and filtering based approaches [12, 30], and alternating optimization of the map and camera poses [34, 16]. More recently, modern SLAM systems have leveraged least-squares optimization. A key element for accuracy has been full Bundle Adjustment (BA), which jointly optimizes the camera poses and the 3D map in a single optimization problem. One advantage of the optimization-based formulation is that a SLAM system can be easily modified to leverage different sensors. For example, ORB-SLAM3 [5] supports monocular, stereo, RGB-D, and IMU sensors, and modern systems can support a variety of camera models [5, 27, 42, 6]. Despite significant progress, current SLAM systems lack the robustness demanded for many real-world applications. Failures come in many forms, such as lost feature tracks, divergence in the optimization algorithm, and accumulation of drift.

Deep learning has been proposed as a solution to many of these failure cases. Previous work has investigated replacing hand-crafted with learned features[13, 7, 29, 26, 35], using neural 3D representations[46, 1, 9, 45, 44, 25, 22], and combining learned energy terms with classical optimization backends[58, 57]. Other work has tried to learn SLAM or VO systems end-to-end [59, 47, 53, 52, 46]. While these systems are sometimes more robust, they fall far short of the accuracy of their classical counterparts on common benchmarks.

In this work we introduce DROID-SLAM, a new SLAM system based on deep learning. It has state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing SLAM systems, classical or learning-based, on challenging benchmarks with very large margins. In particular, it has the following advantages:

- _High Accuracy_ : We achieve large improvements over prior work across multiple datasets and modalities. On the TartanAir SLAM competition [54], we reduce error by 62% over the best prior result on the monocular track and 60% on the stereo track. We rank 1st on the

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

ETH-3D RGB-D SLAM leaderboard [41], outperforming the second place by 35% under the AUC metric which considers both error and rate of catastrophic failure. On EuRoC [2], with monocular input, we reduce error by 82% among methods with zero failures, and by 43% over ORB-SLAM3 considering only the 10 out of 11 sequences it succeeds on. With stereo input, we reduce error by 71% over ORB-SLAM3. On TUM-RGBD [43], we reduce error by 83% among the methods with zero failures.

- _High Robustness_ : We have substantially fewer catastrophic failures than prior systems. On ETH-3D, we successfully track 30 of the 32 RGB-D datasets, while the next best successfully tracks only 19/32. On TartanAir, EuRoC, and TUM-RGBD, we have zero failures.

- _Strong Generalization_ : Our system, trained only with monocular input, can directly use stereo or RGB-D input to get improved accuracy without any retraining. All of our results across 4 datasets and 3 modalities are achieved by a single model, trained once with only monocular input entirely on the synthetic TartanAir dataset.

The strong performance and generalization of DROID-SLAM is made possible by its “Differentiable Recurrent Optimization-Inspired Design” (DROID), which is an end-to-end differentiable architecture that combines the strengths of both classical approaches and deep networks. Specifically, it consists of recurrent iterative updates, building upon RAFT [48] for optical flow but introducing two key innovations.

First, unlike RAFT, which iteratively updates optical flow, we iteratively update camera poses and depth. Whereas RAFT operates on two frames, our updates are applied to an arbitrary number of frames, enabling joint global refinement of all camera poses and depth maps, essential for minimizing drift for long trajectories and loop closures.

Second, each update of camera poses and depth maps in DROID-SLAM is produced by a differentiable Dense Bundle Adjustment (DBA) layer, which computes a Gauss-Newton update to camera poses and _dense per-pixel depth_ so as to maximize their compatibility with the current estimate of optical flow. This DBA layer leverages geometric constraints, improves accuracy and robustness, and enables a monocular system to handle stereo or RGB-D input without retraining.

The design of DROID-SLAM is novel. The closest prior deep architectures are DeepV2D [47] and BA-Net [46], both of which were focused on depth estimation and reported limited SLAM results. DeepV2D alternates between updating depth and updating camera poses, instead of bundle adjustment. BA-Net has a bundle adjustment layer, but their layer is substantially different: it is not “dense” in that it optimizes over a small number of coefficients used to linearly combine a depth basis (a set of pre-predicted depth maps), whereas we optimize over per-pixel depth directly, without being handicapped by a depth basis. In addition, BA-Net optimizes photometric reprojection error (in feature space), whereas we optimize geometric error, leveraging state-of-the-art flow estimation.

We perform extensive evaluation across four different datasets and three different sensor modalities, demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in all cases. We also include ablation studies that shed light on important design decisions and hyperparameters.

## **2 Related Work**

Modern SLAM systems treat localization and mapping as a joint optimization problem [4].

2

**Visual SLAM** focuses on observations in the form of monocular, stereo, or RGB-D images. These approaches are commonly categorized as either being _direct_ or _indirect_ [15]. Indirect approaches [31, 32, 5, 37] first process the image into an intermediate representation by detecting points of interest and attaching feature descriptors. Features are then matched between images. Indirect approaches optimize camera pose and a 3D point cloud by minimizing reprojection error–the distance between a projected 3D point and its location in the image.

Direct approaches model the image formation process and define an objective function over photometric error [16, 15, 60]. One advantage of direct approaches is that they can model more information about the image, such as lines and intensity variations[15] which are not used by indirect approaches. However, photometric errors typically lead to more difficult optimization problems, and direct approaches are less robust to geometric distortion such as rolling shutter artifacts. This approach requires more sophisticated optimization techniques, such as coarse-to-fine image pyramids to avoid local minimum.

Our method does not clearly fit into either of the categories. Like the _direct_ approach, we do not require preprocessing steps to detect and match features between the images. We instead use the full image, allowing us to leverage a wider range of information than indirect methods with typically only use corners and edges. However, we minimize reprojection error similar to indirect methods. This is an easier optimization problem and avoids the need for more complicated representations such as image pyramids. In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.

**Deep Learning** has more recently been applied to the SLAM problem. Many works have focused on training systems for particular subproblems, such as feature detection [13, 7, 29, 26, 35], feature matching and outlier rejection [38, 36], and localization [51, 39]. SuperGlue [38] was designed to perform feature matching and verification and make 2-view pose estimate much more robust. Our network also draws inspiration from Dusmanu et al[14], which builds a neural network into the SfM pipeline to improve keypoint localization accuracy.

Other works have focused on training SLAM systems end-to-end [59, 46, 8, 50, 24, 47, 53]. These methods are not full SLAM systems, but instead focus on small scale reconstruction on the order of two [8, 50, 53] up to a dozen frames [59, 46, 47]. They lack many of the core capabilities of modern SLAM systems such as loop closure and global bundle adjustment which inhibit their ability to perform large scale reconstruction as demonstrated in our experiments. _∇_ SLAM[23] implements several existing SLAM algorithms as differentiable computation graphs, allowing for errors in the reconstruction to be backpropagated back to sensor measurements. While this approach is differentiable, it has no trainable parameters, meaning the performance of the system is limited by the accuracy of the classical algorithm they emulate.

DeepFactors[9] is the most complete deep SLAM system, building on the earlier CodeSLAM[1]. It performs joint optimization of the pose and depth variables, and is capable of short and long-range loop closure. Similar to BA-Net[46], DeepFactors optimizes the parameters of a learned depth basis during inference. In contrast, we do not rely on a learned basis, but instead optimize pixelwise depth. This allows our network to better generalize to new datasets since our depth representation is not tied to the training dataset.

We take a video as input with two objectives: estimate the trajectory of the camera and build a 3D map of the environment. We first describe the monocular setting; in Sec. 3.4 we describe how to generalize the system to stereo and RGB-D video.

## **3.1 Feature Extraction and Correlation**

Features are extracted from each new image added to they system. Key components of this stage are borrowed from RAFT[48].

**Feature Extraction** Each of the input images are processed by a feature extraction network. The network consists of 6 residual blocks and 3 downsampling layers, producing dense feature maps at 1/8 the input image resolution. Like RAFT[48], we use two separate networks: a feature network and a context network. The feature network is used to build the set of correlation volumes, while the context features are injected into the network during each application of the update operator.

**Correlation Pyramid** For each edge in the frame graph, ( _i, j_ ) _∈E_ , we compute a 4D correlation volume by taking the dot product between all-pairs of feature vectors in _gθ_ ( _Ii_ ) and _gθ_ ( _Ij_ )
We then perform average pooling of the last two dimension of the correlation volume following RAFT[48] to form a 4-level correlation pyramid.

**Correlation Lookup** We define a lookup operator which indexes the correlation volume using a grid with radius _r_ , _Lr_ : R _[H][×][W][ ×][H][×][W] ×_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[2] _�→_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[(] _[r]_[+1)][2] .

The lookup operator takes an _H × W_ grid of coordinates as input and values are retrieved from the correlation volume using bilinear interpolation. The operator is applied to each correlation volume in the pyramid and the final feature vector is computed by concatenating the results at each level.

## **3.2 Update Operator**

The core component of our SLAM system is a learned _update operator_ show in Fig. 2. The update operator is a 3 _×_ 3 convolutional GRU with hidden state **h** . Each application of the operator updates the hidden state, and additionally produces a pose update, ∆ _**ξ**_[(] _[k]_[)] , and depth update, ∆ **d**[(] _[k]_[)] . The pose and depth updates are applied to the current depth and pose estimates through retraction on the SE3 manifold and vector addition respectively

Iterative applications of the update operator produce a sequence of poses and depths, with the expectation of converging to a fixed point _{_ **G**[(] _[k]_[)] _} →_ **G** _[∗]_ , _{_ **d**[(] _[k]_[)] _} →_ **d** _[∗]_ , reflecting the true reconstruction.

**Correspondence** At the start of each iteration we use the current estimates of poses and depths to estimate correspondence. Given a grid of pixel coordinates, **p** _i ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[2] in frame _i_ , we compute the dense correspondence field **p** _ij_

for each edge ( _i, j_ ) _∈E_ in the frame graph. Here Π _c_ is the camera model mapping a set of 3D points onto the image and Π _[−] c_[1] is the inverse projection function mapping inverse depth map **d** and coordinate grid **p** _i_ to a 3D point cloud (we provide formulas and Jacobians in the appendix). **p** _ij_ represents the coordinates of pixels **p** _i_ mapped into frame _j_ using the estimated pose and depth.

**Inputs** We use the correspondence field to index the correlation volumes. For each edge ( _i, j_ ) _∈E_ we use **p** _ij_ to perform lookup from the correlation volume **C** _ij_ to retrieve correlation features. Additionally, we use the correspondence field to derive optical flow induced by camera motion as the difference **p** _ij −_ **p** _j_ . Furthermore, the residual from the previous BA solution is concatenated with the flow field allowing the network to use feedback from the previous iteration.

The correlation features provide information about visual similarity in the neighbourhood of **p** _ij_ allowing the network to learn to align visually similar image regions. However, correspondence is sometimes ambiguous. The flow provides an complementary source of information allowing the network to exploit smoothness in the motion fields to gain robustness.

**Update** The correlation features and flow features are each mapped through two convolutional layers before being injected into the GRU. Additionally, we inject context features, as extracted by the context network, into the GRU through element-wise addition.

The ConvGRU is a local operation with a small receptive field. We extract global context by averaging the hidden state across the spatial dimensions of the image and use this feature vector as additional input to the GRU. Global context is important in SLAM because incorrect correspondences, caused by large moving objects for example, can degrade the accuracy of the system. It is important for the network to recognize and reject erroneous correspondence.

The GRU produces an updated hidden state **h**[(] _[k]_[+1)] . Instead of predicting updates to the depth or pose directly, we instead predict updates in the space of dense flow fields. We map the hidden state through two additional convoluation layers to produce two outputs: (1) a revision flow field **r** _ij ∈_ R _[H][×][W][ ×]_[2] and (2) associated confidence map **w** _ij ∈_ R _[H]_ + _[×][W][ ×]_[2] . The revision **r** _ij_ is a correction term predicted by the network to correct errors in the dense correspondence field. We denote the corrected correspondence as **p** _[∗] ij_[=] **[ r]** _[ij]_[+] **[ p]** _[ij]_

We then pool the hidden state over all features which share the same source view _i_ and predict a pixel-wise damping factor _λ_ . We use the softplus operator to ensure that the damping term is positive. Additionally, we use the pooled features to predict a 8x8 mask which can be used to upsample the inverse depth estimate.

## **3.3 Training**

Our SLAM system is implemented in PyTorch and we use the LieTorch extension [49] to perform backprogation in the tangent space of all group elements.

5

**Removing gauge freedom** In the monocular setting, the network is only able to recover the trajectory of the camera up to a similarity transform. One solution is to define a loss which is invariant to similarity transforms. However, the gauge-freedom still exists during training which poorly impacts the conditioning of the linear system and the stability of the gradients. We solve this problem by fixing the first two poses to the ground-truth poses of each training sequence. Fixing the first pose removes the 6-dof gauge freedom. Fixing the second pose resolves the scale freedom.

**Constructing training video** Each training example consists of a 7-frame video sequence. In order to ensure stable training and good downstream performance, we want to sample videos which are not too easy nor too difficult.

The training set is composed of a collection of videos. For each video _i_ of length _Ni_ , we precompute an _Ni × Ni_ distance matrix storing the average optical flow magnitude between each pair of frames. However, not all frames are covisible; and frames pairs with less than 50% overlap are assigned a distance of infinity. During training, we dynamically generate videos by sampling paths in the distance matrix, such that the average flow between adjacent video frames is between 8px and 96px.

**Supervision** We supervise our network using a combination of _pose_ loss and _flow_ loss. The flow loss is applied to pairs of adjacent frames. We compute the optical flow induced by the predicted depth and poses and the flow induced by the ground truth depth and poses. The loss is taken to be the average l2 distance between the two flow fields.

## **3.4 SLAM System**

During inference, we compose the network into a full SLAM system. The SLAM system takes a video stream as input, and performs reconstruction and localization in real-time. Our system contains two threads which run asynchronously. The _frontend_ thread takes in new frames, extracts features, selects keyframes, and performs local bundle adjustment. The _backend_ thread simultaneously performs global bundle adjustment over the entire history of keyframes. We provide an overview of the system here, and provide more information in the appendix.

**Initialization** Initialization is simple with DROID-SLAM. We simply collect frames until we have a set of 12. As we accumulate frames, we only keep the previous frame if optical flow is greater than 16px (estimated by applying one update iteration). Once 12 frames have been accumulated, we initialize a frame graph by creating an edges between keyframes which are within 3 timesteps apart, then run 10 iterations of the update operator.

**Frontend** The frontend operates directly on the incoming video stream. It maintains a collection of keyframes and a frame graph storing edges between covisible kefyrames. Keyframe poses and depths are actively being optimized. Features are first extracted from the incoming frames. The new frame is then added to the frame graph adding edges with its 3 closest neighbors as measured by mean optical flow. The pose is initialized using a linear motion model. We then apply several iterations of the update operator to update keyframe poses and depths. We fix the first two poses to remove gauge freedom but treat all depths as free variables.

After the new frame is tracked, we select a keyframe for removal. We compute distance between pairs of frames by computing the average optical flow magnitude and remove redundant frames. If no frame is a good candidate for removal, we remove the oldest keyframe.

**Backend** The backend performs global bundle adjustment over the entire history of keyframes. During each iteration, we rebuild the frame graph using the flow between all pairs of keyframes, represented as an _N × N_ distance matrix. We first add edges between temporally adjacent keyframes. We then sample new edges from the distance matrix in order of increasing flow. With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs _||_ ( _i, j_ ) _−_ ( _k, l_ ) _||∞_ = max( _|i − k|, |j − l|_ ).

We then apply the update operator to the entire frame graph, often consisting of thousands of frames and edges. Storing the full set of correlation volumes would quickly exceed video memory. Instead, we use the memory efficient implementation proposed in RAFT [48].

Figure 3: DROID-SLAM can generalize to new datasets. In order, we show results from Tanks & Temples [21], ScanNet [10], Sintel [3], and ETH-3D [41]; all using monocular video.

During training, we implement dense bundle adjustment in PyTorch to leverage the automatic differentiation engine. At inference time, we use a custom CUDA kernel which takes advantage of the block-sparse structure of the problem, then perform sparse Cholesky decomposition on the reduced camera block.

We only perform full bundle adjustment on keyframe images. In order to recover the poses of non-keyframes, we perform motion-only bundle adjustment by iteratively estimating flow between each keyframe and its neighboring non-keyframes. During testing, we evaluate on the full camera trajectory, not just keyframes.

**Stereo and RGB-D** Our system can be easily modified for stereo and RGB-D video. In the case of RGB-D, we still treat depth as a variable, since sensor depth can be noisy and have missing observations, and simply add a term to the optimization objective (Eqn. 4) which penalizes the squared distance between the measured and predicted depth. For stereo, we use the exact same system described above, with just double the frames, and fix the relative pose between the left and right frames in the DBA layer. Cross camera edges in the graph allow us to leverage stereo information.

## **4 Experiments**

We experiment on a diverse set of datasets and sensor modalities. We compare to both deep learning and established classical SLAM algorithms and put specific emphasis on cross-dataset generalization. Following prior work, we evaluate the accuracy of the camera trajectory [31, 15, 41], primarily using Absolute Trajectory Error (ATE) [43]. While some datasets have ground truth point clouds [21], there is no standard protocol to compare 3D reconstructions directly given by SLAM systems because a SLAM systems can choose which 3D points to reconstruct. Evaluating dense 3D reconstruction is typically considered in the domain of Multiview Stereo [19] and outside the scope of this work.

Our network is trained entirely on monocular video from the synthetic TartanAir dataset [54]. We train our network for 250k steps with a batch size of 4, resolution 384 _×_ 512, and 7 frame clips, and unroll 15 update iterations. Training takes 1 week on 4 RTX-3090 GPUs.

Table 1: Results on the TartanAir monocular benchmark.

**TartanAir [54] (Monocular & Stereo)** The TartanAir dataset is a challenging synthetic benchmark for evaluating SLAM algorithms and was used as part of the ECCV 2020 SLAM competition. We use the official test split [54], and provide ATE across all “Hard” sequences in Tab. 1.

Tab. 1 demonstrates both the robustness of our method (no catastrophic failures) and accuracy (very low drift). We retrain DeepV2D [47] on TartanAir as a baseline. On most sequences, we outperform existing methods by an order-of-magnitude and achieve 8x lower average error than TartanVO [53] and 20x lower than DeepV2D [47]. We also use the TartanAir dataset to compare with the top submissions to the ECCV 2020 SLAM competition in Tab. 2. The top two submissions use systems

**EuRoC [2] (Monocular & Stereo)** In the remaining experiments, we are interested in the ability of our network to generalize to new cameras and environments. The EuRoC dataset consists of video captured from sensor on-board a micro aerial vehicle (MAV) and is a widely used benchmark to evaluate SLAM systems. We use the EuRoC dataset to evaluate both monocular and stereo performance and report results on Tab. 3.

In the monocular setting, we achieve an average ATE of 2.2cm, reducing error by 82% among methods with zero failures, and by 43% over ORB-SLAM3 when only comparing sequences where ORB-SLAM3 is successful.

We compare to several deep learning approaches. We compare to DeepV2D trained on the TartanAir dataset and the publicly available version trained on NYUv2 [33] and ScanNet[10]. DeepFactors [9] was trained on ScanNet. We find that recent deep learning approaches [9, 47, 53] perform poorly on the EuRoC dataset compared to classical SLAM systems. This is due to poor generalization and dataset biases which lead to large amounts of drift; our method does not suffer from these issues. D3VO [57] is able to achieve both good robustness and accuracy by combining a neural network frontend with DSO as a backend, using 6 of the 11 sequences for evaluation and performing unsupervised training on the remaining ones, which contain the same scenes used for evaluation.

**TUM-RGBD [43]** The RGBD dataset consists of indoor scenes captured with handheld camera. This is a notoriously difficult dataset for monocular methods due to rolling shutter artifacts, motion blur, and heavy rotation. We benchmark prior work on the entirety of the freiburg1 set in Tab. 4.

**ETH3D-SLAM [41] (RGB-D)** Finally, we evaluate the RGB-D performance on the ETH3D-SLAM benchmark. In this setup, the network is also provided measurements from an RGB-D camera. We take our network trained on TartanAir and add an addition term in the optimization objective penalizing the distance between the predicted inv. depth and inv. depth measured by the sensor. Without any finetuning, our method ranks 1st on both the train and test splits. Several of the datasets are "dark" meaning no image data is available; on these datasets we do not submit any predictions. On the test set, we successfully track 30/32 RGB-D, improving over the next best of 19/32.

**Timing and Memory** Our system can run in real-time with 2 3090 GPUs. Tracking and local BA is run on the first GPU, while global BA and loop closure is run on the second. On EuRoC, we average 20fps (camera hz) by downsampling to 320 _×_ 512 resolution and skipping every other frame. Results in Tab. 3 were obtained in this setting. On TUM-RGBD, we average 30fps by downsampling to 240 _×_ 320 and skipping every other frame, again the reported results where obtained in this setting. On TartanAir, due to much faster camera motion, we are unable to run in real-time, averaging 8fps. However, this is still a 16x speedup over the top 2 submissions to the TartanAir SLAM challenge, which rely on COLMAP [40].

The SLAM frontend can be run on GPUs with 8GB of memory. The backend, which requires storing feature maps from the full set of images, is more memory intensive. All results on TUM-RGBD can be produced on a single 1080Ti graphics card. Results on EuRoC, TartanAir and ETH-3D (where video can be up to 5000 frames) requires a GPU with 24GB memory. While memory and resource requirements are currently the biggest limitation of our system, we believe these can be drastically reduced by culling redundant computation and more efficient representations.

## **5 Conclusion**

We introduce DROID-SLAM, an end-to-end neural architecture for visual SLAM. DROID-SLAM is accurate, robust, and versatile and can be used on monocular, stereo, and RGB-D video. It outperforms prior work by large margins on challenging benchmarks.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a significant advancement in deep visual SLAM, introducing a novel differentiable architecture that combines iterative refinement with dense bundle adjustment. It demonstrates impressive performance gains over both classical and learning-based methods across multiple benchmarks and sensor modalities. The core idea of using a recurrent update operator with a differentiable geometric optimization layer is conceptually strong and well-motivated.

Overview issues:

- Inadequate analysis of generalization under domain shift: The paper claims strong generalization, particularly training on synthetic TartanAir data and testing on real-world datasets like EuRoC and TUM-RGBD. However, it lacks a systematic analysis of failure modes or performance degradation under significant domain shifts—such as extreme lighting, motion blur, or novel scene dynamics. While cross-dataset results are reported, the absence of ablation on environmental stressors (e.g., low texture, reflective surfaces) limits confidence in real-world robustness. Readers will wonder how the system behaves when photometric assumptions break down or when optical flow becomes unreliable. The authors should include a failure mode analysis or sensitivity study to clarify the boundaries of generalization.

- Missing evaluation of long-term drift and loop closure effectiveness: Although the backend performs global bundle adjustment and the paper reports low ATE, there is no detailed analysis of how well loop closures are detected and corrected over long trajectories. The frame graph construction relies on optical flow magnitude to add long-range edges, but this heuristic may fail in perceptually aliased environments or when revisiting scenes after significant appearance change. The ETH-3D results show high success rates, but without trajectory plots or drift-over-length analysis, it's unclear whether global consistency is truly maintained. A natural question is whether the low ATE stems from accurate loop closure or simply low short-term drift. The paper would benefit from visualizing corrected trajectories before and after global BA on large loops.

- Overstated real-time performance claims: The paper states the system runs in real-time (20–30 fps), but this is achieved only after downsampling to 320×512 or 240×320 and skipping every other frame—effectively operating at 10–15 Hz on fast sequences. On full-resolution TartanAir, it averages 8 fps, which is below real-time. These conditions are not sufficiently emphasized, potentially misleading readers about deployability. Moreover, the two-GPU setup (one for frontend, one for backend) is non-trivial for embedded robotics. The real-time claim needs qualification: it applies only under aggressive resolution and frame-rate reduction. The authors should clarify the trade-off between accuracy and latency under different operating conditions.

- Insufficient justification for monocular training with stereo/RGB-D inference: The paper highlights that a model trained solely on monocular synthetic data can use stereo or RGB-D inputs at test time without retraining. While this is impressive, the mechanism is only briefly explained—adding a depth consistency term in the DBA layer. However, no ablation is provided to show how much of the improvement comes from this term versus the geometric flow-based updates. Does the network actually learn to downweight noisy sensor depth? How does it handle calibration mismatch? This generalization claim would be stronger with a controlled study isolating the contribution of sensor fusion in the optimization step.

- Lack of dense reconstruction quality assessment: The paper explicitly states that dense 3D reconstruction is outside its scope, focusing only on trajectory accuracy. However, DROID-SLAM outputs per-pixel inverse depth, and the method is compared to systems that produce dense maps. Readers will question whether the depth estimates are geometrically accurate or just sufficient for pose refinement. Without evaluation on depth accuracy (e.g., against ground truth depth in TUM-RGB-D or ETH-3D), the utility of the 'dense map' component remains unverified. A minimal fix would be to report depth RMSE on available ground truth sequences, even if not the main contribution.

- Memory scalability not addressed despite being a known limitation: The paper acknowledges high memory usage (24GB GPU for long sequences) as the 'biggest limitation' but offers no architectural or algorithmic mitigation. Given that dense correlation volumes and long frame graphs are central to the method, the lack of discussion on memory-efficient alternatives—such as sparse approximations, caching strategies, or temporal summarization—undermines the practicality claim. Subsequent work like DPV-SLAM already addresses this, so the omission here makes the system appear less forward-looking. The authors should at least discuss possible avenues for reducing memory footprint, even if not implemented.

- No ablation isolating depth fusion gain in RGB-D mode: The paper claims the system improves with RGB-D input by adding a sensor depth consistency term but provides no ablation to quantify its contribution. Without comparing performance with and without the depth term on the same sequences, it is unclear how much of the accuracy gain comes from sensor fusion versus flow-based pose refinement. This matters because the core claim of zero-shot generalization to RGB-D hinges on effective depth integration. The authors should report ATE and depth RMSE on ETH-3D or TUM-RGB-D sequences using two variants: one with the depth term disabled (relying only on flow) and one with it enabled, showing the delta in trajectory and map accuracy.

- Missing long-term drift analysis despite global BA: While the paper reports low ATE, it does not analyze how drift accumulates over trajectory length or how effectively loop closures correct it. For a system emphasizing global bundle adjustment, this is a notable gap. Readers will want to know if accuracy is maintained over kilometer-scale sequences or if errors grow sublinearly. The authors should plot ATE versus path length on long sequences (e.g., EuRoC V1_03 or ETH-3D train loop) and show trajectory overlays before and after global BA to demonstrate actual loop closure correction, not just endpoint accuracy.

- No evaluation of depth map quality despite dense output: The method optimizes per-pixel inverse depth, yet the paper avoids evaluating depth accuracy, stating dense reconstruction is out of scope. But since depth is a core state variable and the DBA layer directly updates it, failing to assess its fidelity undermines the claim of dense 3D mapping. This is especially important when using noisy RGB-D input—does the system refine or degrade sensor depth? The authors should compute depth RMSE against ground truth on TUM-RGB-D or ETH-3D sequences and include visualizations of refined depth maps versus raw sensor input.

- Unverified generalization to real-world stereo input: The paper claims the monocular-trained model works on stereo without retraining but only evaluates stereo on synthetic TartanAir. It does not test on real stereo datasets like KITTI or EuRoC stereo, where calibration error, rectification artifacts, or sensor noise could break assumptions. This limits confidence in cross-modal generalization. The authors should report ATE on EuRoC stereo sequences using the same monocular-trained model and compare against ORB-SLAM3 stereo to validate the claim under real-world conditions.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include a failure mode analysis or sensitivity study to demonstrate robustness under domain shift and environmental stressors

- Provide detailed evaluation of loop closure performance, including trajectory plots before and after global bundle adjustment on long sequences

- Clarify real-time performance claims by reporting effective frame rate, resolution, and hardware requirements under different operating modes

- Add ablation or analysis showing the contribution of sensor fusion (e.g., RGB-D term) when using stereo/depth inputs at test time

- Report depth accuracy metrics on datasets with ground truth depth (e.g., TUM-RGB-D) to validate the quality of dense reconstruction

comments.title: Misleading claim about reprojection error in method classification
comments.quote: However, we minimize reprojection error similar to indirect methods. This is an easier optimization problem and avoids the need for more complicated representations such as image pyramids.
comments.explanation: The method does not minimize reprojection error, as no explicit 3D points are tracked or matched. Instead, the loss is defined over photometric consistency via warped image intensities, making it a direct method. Reprojection error requires known 3D-to-2D correspondences, which are absent in DROID-SLAM's joint depth and pose estimation. Claiming otherwise misrepresents the core optimization and creates a false hybrid categorization. The comparison to indirect methods should be removed, and the method should be correctly classified as a direct approach with learned updates.

comments.title: Incorrect stereo input handling via temporal frame doubling
comments.quote: For stereo, we use the exact same system described above, with just double the frames, and fix the relative pose between the left and right frames in the DBA layer.
comments.explanation: Treating left and right stereo images as consecutive temporal frames misaligns the system's recurrent update mechanism, which assumes motion over time. This would corrupt the GRU's hidden state propagation and lead to erroneous pose estimates. Stereo pairs should be processed as a single multi-view observation at the same timestamp, not as sequential frames. The current description risks implementation errors and undermines the zero-shot generalization claim. Clarify that stereo inputs are handled as synchronized dual-camera observations within a single time step, not as a temporal sequence.

comments.title: Unjustified addition of depth consistency term in RGB-D mode
comments.quote: In the case of RGB-D, we still treat depth as a variable, since sensor depth can be noisy and have missing observations, and simply add a term to the optimization objective (Eqn. 4) which penalizes the squared distance between the measured and predicted depth.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'simply add a term' glosses over critical issues in joint optimization: gradient scaling, residual weighting, and convergence stability. Photometric and depth residuals operate on different scales (pixel intensity vs. meters), and without proper normalization or learned weighting, the depth term could dominate or destabilize the optimization. Moreover, the network was trained on monocular data without such a term, so its behavior under this new residual is undefined. The zero-shot generalization claim requires justification of how the added term is balanced—either through explicit normalization, annealing, or learned confidence. Replace 'simply' with a precise description of the modified objective and its integration into the optimization.

comments.title: Contradictory evaluation claim for EuRoC stereo
comments.quote: We use the EuRoC dataset to evaluate both monocular and stereo performance and report results on Tab. 3.
comments.explanation: Table 3 is explicitly labeled 'Monocular SLAM on the EuRoC datasets' and contains no stereo results, contradicting the claim of stereo evaluation. The paper provides no ATE values for EuRoC stereo, despite asserting such evaluation. This undermines the cross-modal generalization argument, especially since real-world stereo data (unlike synthetic TartanAir) would test calibration robustness. Either present stereo results or revise the claim to reflect that only monocular performance is evaluated on EuRoC. The current wording misleads readers about the scope of validation.

comments.title: Inconsistent real-time performance claim
comments.quote: d Memory Our system can run in real-time with 2 3090 GPUs. Tracking and local BA is run on the first GPU, while global BA and loop closure is run on the second. On EuRoC, we average 20fps (camera hz) by downsampling to  $320 \times 512$  resolution and skipping every other frame. Results i
comments.explanation: The 'real-time' claim is misleading: skipping every other frame means the system processes input at half the sensor rate (e.g., 10 Hz on a 20 Hz sequence), risking motion blur and tracking failure. True real-time SLAM should process or meaningfully interpolate every frame. Additionally, the two-GPU setup is impractical for embedded deployment. The claim should be qualified to state that the system achieves 20 Hz on downsampled, decimated input using two GPUs—not full real-time operation on original data.

comments.title: Overstated conclusion on benchmark performance across modalities
comments.quote: DROID-SLAM is accurate, robust, and versatile and can be used on monocular, stereo, and RGB-D video. It outperforms prior work by large margins on challenging benchmarks.
comments.explanation: The conclusion generalizes performance gains to all modalities, but the evaluation lacks real-world stereo (e.g., KITTI, EuRoC stereo) and real RGB-D sequences beyond synthetic TartanAir. The stereo and RGB-D results are confined to synthetic data, limiting support for broad claims about real-world applicability. While monocular performance on real datasets is strong, the cross-modal generalization is not sufficiently validated. Revise the conclusion to clarify that stereo and RGB-D improvements are demonstrated primarily in simulation.

comments.title: Incorrect correlation volume formulation
comments.quote:
For each edge in the frame graph, $(i,j) \in \mathcal{E}$, we compute a 4D correlation volume by taking the dot product between all-pairs of feature vectors in $g_{\theta}(I_i)$ and $g_{\theta}(I_j)$

$$
C _ {u _ {1} v _ {1} u _ {2} v _ {2}} ^ {i j} = \left\langle g _ {\theta} \left(I _ {i}\right) _ {u _ {1} v _ {1}}, g _ {\theta} \left(I _ {j}\right) _ {u _ {2} v _ {2}} \right\rangle \tag {1}
$$
comments.explanation: The correlation volume as defined indexes over absolute pixel coordinates in both images, resulting in a 4D tensor of size $H \times W \times H \times W$. This is computationally infeasible and inconsistent with RAFT, which uses relative displacements within a local window. The correct form should be $C_{u v \Delta u \Delta v}$, correlating features at $(u,v)$ in frame $i$ with those at $(u+\Delta u, v+\Delta v)$ in frame $j$. The current definition would require prohibitive memory and does not match standard practice. Revise the equation to reflect relative offsets and align with efficient correlation pyramid implementations.

comments.title: Ambiguous loop closure trigger mechanism
comments.quote: If the camera returns to a previously mapped region, we add long range connections in the graph to perform loop closure.
comments.explanation: The condition for detecting 'return to a previously mapped region' is unspecified—no descriptor, similarity threshold, or geometric verification method is mentioned. This critical step for global consistency is underspecified and not reproducible. Readers cannot implement or evaluate the loop closure logic without knowing how revisitation is detected. Clarify whether visual retrieval (e.g., NetVLAD), pose prediction, or geometric registration triggers long-range edge addition, and specify any thresholds used.

comments.title: Additive inverse depth update risks violating positivity
comments.quote:
The pose and depth updates are applied to the current depth and pose estimates through retraction on the SE3 manifold and vector addition respectively

$$
\mathbf {G} ^ {(k + 1)} = \operatorname {E x p} \left(\Delta \boldsymbol {\xi} ^ {(k)}\right) \circ \mathbf {G} ^ {(k)}, \quad \mathbf {d} ^ {(k + 1)} = \Delta \mathbf {d} ^ {(k)} + \mathbf {d} ^ {(k)}. \tag {2}
$$
comments.explanation: The update $\mathbf{d}^{(k+1)} = \Delta \mathbf{d}^{(k)} + \mathbf{d}^{(k)}$ risks producing non-positive inverse depth values, which would correspond to invalid or infinite depth. While the domain is specified as $\mathbb{R}_+$, no activation function (e.g., softplus, exponential) is mentioned to enforce positivity. In contrast, the pose update uses SE(3) retraction, preserving geometric structure. A multiplicative update like $\mathbf{d}^{(k+1)} = \mathbf{d}^{(k)} \odot \exp(\Delta \mathbf{d}^{(k)})$ would naturally preserve positivity and improve numerical stability.

comments.title: Diagonal Hessian claim relies on unacknowledged approximation
comments.quote: where $\mathbf{C}$ is diagonal and can be cheaply inverted $\mathbf{C}^{-1}=1/\mathbf{C}$. The DBA layer is implemented as part of the computation graph and backpropogation is performed through the layer during training.
comments.explanation: The block $\mathbf{C}$ in the Gauss-Newton system corresponds to the depth Hessian, which is not inherently diagonal—shared depth variables across multiple edges introduce off-diagonal terms. The diagonality arises only due to the pixelwise damping factor $\lambda$, which regularizes the system. This approximation should be explicitly acknowledged. Revise to state that $\mathbf{C}$ is approximated as diagonal via $\lambda$, enabling efficient inversion, rather than implying structural diagonality.

comments.title: Misuse of Chebyshev distance for edge suppression in frame graph
comments.quote: With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs $||(i,j)-(k,l)||_{\infty}=\max(|i-k|,|j-l|)$.
comments.explanation: Defining Chebyshev distance on edge index pairs $(i,j)$ is ill-posed—the frame graph does not have a grid structure, and edge indices are arbitrary. Suppressing edges based on array index proximity risks excluding geometrically useful long-range matches. The intended mechanism likely operates on frame timestamps or vertex indices. Rewrite using vertex-based suppression: e.g., suppress edges incident to frames within temporal distance 2 of $i$ or $j$.

comments.title: Ambiguous recovery of non-keyframe poses
comments.quote: In order to recover the poses of non-keyframes, we perform motion-only bundle adjustment by iteratively estimating flow between each keyframe and its neighboring non-keyframes.
comments.explanation: The term 'motion-only bundle adjustment' is misleading—DROID-SLAM jointly optimizes pose and depth, and 'estimating flow' suggests a photometric process, not a geometric BA step. It is unclear whether this is a learned update or a classical optimization, and how it integrates with the recurrent refinement. Clarify whether non-keyframe poses are interpolated, regressed via the network, or optimized in a separate step, and specify the variables and residuals involved.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of DeepFactors' depth representation
comments.quote: Similar to BA-Net*[46]*, DeepFactors optimizes the parameters of a learned depth basis during inference. In contrast, we do not rely on a learned basis, but instead optimize pixelwise depth.
comments.explanation: This misrepresents DeepFactors: it models per-pixel inverse depth as a sparse Gaussian process using inducing points, not a global low-rank basis like BA-Net. The method still optimizes dense depth maps and does not compress depth into a fixed low-dimensional space. The contrast overstates DROID-SLAM's novelty in depth parameterization. Revise to accurately reflect that both methods optimize dense depth, with differences in regularization, not representation.

comments.title: Incorrect average ATE claim in TartanAir table
comments.explanation: The average ATE of 0.24 is numerically correct (mean of listed values is ~0.235), but the entry for MH005 (1.31 m) is an outlier—over an order of magnitude higher than others—despite claims of 'no catastrophic failures' and 'very low drift'. Given that TartanVO reports 3.19 on MH005 and DROID-SLAM is said to outperform it by 8x, a value of 1.31 is implausible. This suggests a likely transcription error (e.g., 0.131 intended). Either correct the value or justify the anomaly in the text.

comments.title: Misleading speed comparison to competition submissions
comments.quote: Our method, on the other hand, runs 16x faster and achieves an error  $62\%$  lower on the monocular benchmark and  $60\%$  lower on the stereo benchmark.
comments.explanation: The '16x faster' claim is inconsistent with reported speeds: the paper states TartanAir runs at 8 fps, while competition methods run at ~0.75 fps (40x slower than real-time), implying an ~10.7x speedup, not 16x. The ambiguity in '16x faster'—whether relative to real-time or the competition—creates confusion. Clarify by stating actual speeds: 'Our method runs at 8 fps vs. ~0.75 fps for COLMAP-based submissions, achieving an 11x speedup and 62% lower error.'

comments.title: Misuse of RAFT's context feature injection mechanism
comments.quote: Like RAFT[48], we use two separate networks: a feature network and a context network. The feature network is used to build the set of correlation volumes, while the context features are injected into the network during each application of the update operator.
comments.explanation: RAFT uses context features only to initialize the GRU's hidden state, not to inject at every update step. The quoted text implies repeated injection, which is not RAFT's design. If DROID-SLAM modifies this, it must specify how. Otherwise, the claim misrepresents RAFT. Revise to state that context features initialize the GRU state, consistent with RAFT, or explicitly describe any deviation.

comments.title: Ambiguous correlation lookup operator definition
comments.quote: We define a lookup operator which indexes the correlation volume using a grid with radius $r$, $L_r: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times H \times W} \times \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 2} \mapsto \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times (r + 1)^2}$.
comments.explanation: The domain assumes an absolute correlation volume ($H \times W \times H \times W$), but RAFT uses relative displacements ($H \times W \times (2r+1)^2$). The output dimension $(r+1)^2$ suggests a non-symmetric window, unlike standard $(2r+1)^2$ lookup. Bilinear interpolation over absolute coordinates is ill-defined without resolving the second pixel pair. Correct the operator to reflect relative correlation volumes and symmetric windows.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
DROID-SLAM presents a novel deep learning approach to visual SLAM that achieves impressive accuracy across multiple benchmarks. The system's core innovation is a differentiable Dense Bundle Adjustment layer that enables joint optimization of poses and depth maps. While the empirical results are strong, the paper has several significant methodological gaps that need to be addressed, particularly regarding computational efficiency claims, robustness evaluation, and the training-to-deployment pipeline.

The paper makes a substantial contribution by demonstrating that deep learning can achieve state-of-the-art results in visual SLAM, outperforming both classical and learning-based methods by large margins. The differentiable DBA layer is a clever architectural innovation that effectively bridges geometric optimization with learning. The cross-modal generalization from monocular training to stereo/RGB-D deployment is particularly impressive. However, the paper falls short in several critical areas: the computational efficiency analysis is incomplete, robustness to challenging conditions is not systematically evaluated, and key implementation details are missing.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient Computational Efficiency Analysis: The paper claims real-time performance but provides inadequate analysis. Section 4 reports 20-30fps only after significant downsampling (320x512 or 240x320) and frame skipping, yet Table 3 results are obtained at these reduced resolutions. The memory requirements are severe - needing 24GB GPUs for longer sequences, which contradicts real-time deployment claims. More critically, the paper doesn't analyze how performance scales with sequence length or graph size. For a SLAM system, computational complexity should grow gracefully, but the current implementation appears to have super-linear memory growth. The paper should include detailed runtime profiling showing per-component costs (feature extraction, correlation, DBA) and how they scale with graph size and resolution.

- Missing Robustness Evaluation on Challenging Conditions: Despite claiming high robustness, the paper lacks systematic evaluation on challenging conditions mentioned in the domain calibration. The abstract states the system suffers from "substantially fewer catastrophic failures," but there's no dedicated analysis of failure modes. Specifically missing: (1) Performance on textureless surfaces where feature matching typically fails, (2) Robustness to motion blur and rolling shutter artifacts (briefly mentioned for TUM-RGBD but not quantified), (3) Behavior under varying lighting conditions, (4) Handling of dynamic scenes with moving objects. The paper should include controlled experiments on these failure modes, perhaps using synthetic data where ground truth is available, to substantiate the robustness claims.

- Inadequate Ablation Study of Key Components: The paper lacks a comprehensive ablation study to justify its architectural choices. While Section 4 mentions "ablation studies that shed light on important design decisions," the provided content doesn't show these results. Critical missing ablations include: (1) The contribution of the DBA layer vs. a simpler update mechanism, (2) The effect of the correlation pyramid depth and lookup radius, (3) The impact of the global context averaging in the GRU, (4) The necessity of the flow revision mechanism vs. direct pose/depth prediction. Without these ablations, it's unclear which components are essential for the performance gains. The paper should include a systematic ablation table showing the impact of each major component on accuracy and runtime.

- Unclear Training-to-Deployment Pipeline Details: The paper doesn't sufficiently explain how the training procedure translates to real-world deployment. Section 3.3 describes training on 7-frame clips, but Section 3.4 describes a SLAM system that processes thousands of frames. There's a critical gap in explaining how the network, trained on short clips, generalizes to long sequences. The paper mentions fixing the first two poses during training to remove gauge freedom, but doesn't specify how this is handled during deployment on new sequences. Additionally, the training uses synthetic TartanAir data exclusively, but the paper doesn't discuss potential domain shift issues or why this generalizes so well to real datasets. The authors should provide more details on the training strategy and include analysis of performance as a function of training data diversity.

- Limited Loop Closure and Global Consistency Analysis: The paper claims to handle loop closures through the frame graph (Section 3.4), but provides no quantitative analysis of loop closure performance. This is a critical omission since loop closure detection and global consistency are fundamental capabilities for SLAM systems. The paper should report: (1) Loop closure detection rate on sequences with known revisits, (2) Accuracy improvement from loop closures, (3) Computational overhead of adding long-range edges to the graph. The ETH3D results mention successful tracking on 30/32 sequences but don't break down performance by whether loops were present. Without this analysis, it's unclear whether DROID-SLAM truly solves the global consistency problem or merely performs well on trajectories without significant loops.

- Missing failure mode analysis: The paper claims substantially fewer catastrophic failures but lacks systematic analysis of failure modes. While results show zero failures on some datasets, there's no examination of what conditions cause failures when they do occur. The paper should include a dedicated section analyzing failure cases across all datasets, categorizing them by type (e.g., textureless surfaces, extreme motion, dynamic objects), and demonstrating how DROID-SLAM handles these challenging scenarios compared to baselines. This would substantiate the robustness claims and help understand the system's limitations.

- Insufficient ablation of DBA layer: The paper introduces the Dense Bundle Adjustment layer as a key innovation but doesn't adequately demonstrate its necessity. There's no comparison against a simplified version without DBA or with alternative update mechanisms. The paper should include an ablation showing: (1) Performance with DBA vs. direct pose/depth prediction, (2) Effect of the Gauss-Newton update vs. simpler gradient descent, (3) Impact of the confidence-weighted formulation. This would clarify whether DBA is essential for the claimed performance gains or if simpler alternatives could achieve similar results.

- No loop closure quantitative analysis: Despite claiming to handle loop closures through the frame graph, the paper provides no quantitative evaluation of loop closure performance. This is a critical gap since loop closure is fundamental to SLAM. The paper should report: (1) Loop closure detection rate on sequences with known revisits, (2) Trajectory accuracy improvement before vs. after loop closure, (3) Computational overhead of adding long-range edges. Without this analysis, it's unclear whether DROID-SLAM truly achieves global consistency or merely performs well on trajectories without significant loops.

- Missing computational scaling analysis: The paper claims real-time performance but doesn't analyze how computational requirements scale with sequence length. Memory usage grows to 24GB for longer sequences, suggesting super-linear scaling. The paper should include: (1) Runtime and memory as functions of graph size, (2) Per-component timing breakdown (feature extraction, correlation, DBA), (3) Analysis of how frame skipping and downsampling affect accuracy. This is essential for understanding practical deployment constraints and whether the system can handle truly long-term SLAM scenarios.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive computational efficiency analysis including runtime profiling at full resolution and memory scaling with sequence length

- Include systematic robustness evaluation on textureless surfaces, motion blur, varying lighting, and dynamic scenes with quantitative failure analysis

- Provide complete ablation study of all major architectural components (DBA layer, correlation pyramid, global context, flow revision)

- Clarify the training-to-deployment pipeline and analyze how short-clip training generalizes to long sequences

- Add dedicated loop closure evaluation with detection rates and accuracy improvements on benchmark sequences with known revisits

comments.title: Inconsistent ORB-SLAM3 comparison on EuRoC
comments.quote: On EuRoC [2], with monocular input, we reduce error by  $82\%$  among methods with zero failures, and by  $43\%$  over ORB-SLAM3 considering only the 10 out of 11 sequences it succeeds on. With stereo input, we reduce error by  $71\%$  over ORB-SLAM3.
comments.explanation: The comparison methodology is inconsistent between the monocular and stereo cases. For monocular input, the paper reports two different error reductions: 82% compared to other zero-failure methods, and 43% compared to ORB-SLAM3 on only the sequences where ORB-SLAM3 succeeded. For stereo input, only the 71% reduction over ORB-SLAM3 is reported, but it's unclear whether this comparison uses all sequences or only the successful ones like the monocular case. This inconsistency makes it difficult to fairly assess the relative performance. The paper should clarify whether the 71% stereo reduction follows the same methodology as the 43% monocular reduction (comparing only on successful ORB-SLAM3 sequences) or uses a different baseline.

comments.title: Inconsistent ATE calculation in Table 2
comments.quote: Table 2: Results on the TartanAir test set, compared with the top 3 submission to the ECCV 2020 SLAM competition. The score is computed using normalized relative pose error for all possible sequences of length  $\{5,10,15,\dots,40\}$  meters, see competition page for details.
comments.explanation: There's a critical inconsistency in how results are reported. Table 2 claims to use normalized relative pose error for sequences of length {5,10,15,...,40} meters, but the table header and abstract discussion refer to ATE (Absolute Trajectory Error). These are different metrics with different properties. The paper should clarify whether Table 2 uses ATE or the competition's normalized relative pose error metric, and ensure consistent reporting throughout. If it uses the competition metric, the comparison to ATE results in other tables is not directly meaningful.

comments.title: Inconsistent correlation volume dimensions
comments.quote: We define a lookup operator which indexes the correlation volume using a grid with radius $r$, $L_r: \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times H \times W} \times \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 2} \mapsto \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times (r + 1)^2}$.
comments.explanation: There's an inconsistency in the correlation volume dimensions. The lookup operator signature shows the correlation volume as $\mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times H \times W}$, but earlier the text states that features are extracted at 1/8 resolution. If the input images are at resolution $(H, W)$, then the feature maps should be at $(H/8, W/8)$, making the correlation volume $\mathbb{R}^{H/8 \times W/8 \times H/8 \times W/8}$. The current notation suggests the correlation volume maintains full resolution, which contradicts the earlier statement about 1/8 resolution feature extraction.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in DBA equations
comments.quote:
h updates. We define the cost function over the entire frame graph

$\mathbf{E}(\mathbf{G}^{\prime},\mathbf{d}^{\prime})=\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}}\left\|\mathbf{p}_{ij}^{*}-\Pi_{c}(\mathbf{G}_{ij}^{\prime}\circ\Pi_{c}^{-1}(\mathbf{p}_{i},\mathbf{d}_{i}^{\prime}))\right\|_{\Sigma_{ij}}^{2}\qquad\Sigma_{ij}=\operatorname{diag}\mathbf{w}_{ij}.$ (4)
comments.explanation:
The notation inconsistency is valid. Eq. 4 defines the cost function in terms of primed variables $\mathbf{G}^{\prime},\mathbf{d}^{\prime}$ as optimization targets, but Eq. 5 solves for updates $\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi},\Delta\mathbf{d}$ to be applied to current estimates $\mathbf{G}^{(k)},\mathbf{d}^{(k)}$. The connection between these formulations is unclear. The cost function should be expressed directly in terms of the update variables to match the Gauss-Newton solution.

Rewrite Eq. 4 as: $\mathbf{E}(\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi},\Delta\mathbf{d})=\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}}\left\|\mathbf{p}_{ij}^{*}-\Pi_{c}(\mathbf{G}_{ij}^{(k)}\circ\operatorname{Exp}(\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi}_{ij})\circ\Pi_{c}^{-1}(\mathbf{p}_{i},\mathbf{d}_{i}^{(k)}+\Delta\mathbf{d}_{i}))\right\|_{\Sigma_{ij}}^{2}$ because this makes explicit that the optimization variables are the updates applied to current estimates.

comments.title: Missing gauge freedom handling in optimization
comments.quote:
define the cost function over the entire frame graph

$\mathbf{E}(\mathbf{G}^{\prime},\mathbf{d}^{\prime})=\sum_{(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}}\left\|\mathbf{p}_{ij}^{*}-\Pi_{c}(\mathbf{G}_{ij}^{\prime}\circ\Pi_{c}^{-1}(\mathbf{p}_{i},\mathbf{d}_{i}^{\prime}))\right\|_{\Sigma_{ij}}^{2}\qquad\Sigma_{ij}=\operatorname{diag}\mathbf{w}_{ij}.$ (4)
comments.explanation:
The gauge freedom issue is valid. The cost function in Eq. 4 is invariant to global SE(3) transformations of all poses and corresponding depth adjustments, making the Hessian singular. The Schur complement solution in Eq. 5 requires invertible matrices, which is impossible without gauge constraints. The paper doesn't specify how this singularity is resolved in the DBA layer.

Add after Eq. 4: "To resolve gauge freedom, we fix the first pose $\mathbf{G}_0$ to the identity transformation and constrain the first depth map $\mathbf{d}_0$ to remain constant during optimization, ensuring the Hessian remains positive definite" because this provides the necessary constraints for a well-posed optimization problem.

comments.title: Undefined correlation volume indexing
comments.quote: Inputs We use the correspondence field to index the correlation volumes. For each edge $(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$ we use $\mathbf{p}_{ij}$ to perform lookup from the correlation volume $\mathbf{C}_{ij}$ to retrieve correlation features.
comments.explanation:
The paper doesn't define how the correlation volume $\mathbf{C}_{ij}$ is constructed or indexed. The correspondence field $\mathbf{p}_{ij}$ contains 2D pixel coordinates, but correlation volumes typically require 4D indexing (2D coordinates in both source and target frames). The text doesn't explain how the 2D coordinates $\mathbf{p}_{ij}$ are used to index what appears to be a 4D volume, nor does it specify the resolution or structure of $\mathbf{C}_{ij}$.

Add after this sentence: "The correlation volume $\mathbf{C}_{ij}$ is constructed as a 4D tensor of size $H \times W \times H \times W$ where $\mathbf{C}_{ij}[u,v,u',v']$ measures the similarity between pixel $(u,v)$ in frame $i$ and pixel $(u',v')$ in frame $j$. We perform bilinear sampling using $\mathbf{p}_{ij}$ to extract features along the corresponding 2D slice" because this clarifies the indexing mechanism.

comments.title: Inconsistent keyframe evaluation claim
comments.quote: We only perform full bundle adjustment on keyframe images. In order to recover the poses of non-keyframes, we perform motion-only bundle adjustment by iteratively estimating flow between each keyframe and its neighboring non-keyframes. During testing, we evaluate on the full camera trajectory, not just keyframes.
comments.explanation: The text creates a logical inconsistency. It states that full bundle adjustment is only performed on keyframes, and non-keyframes are recovered through motion-only bundle adjustment. However, it then claims to evaluate on the full camera trajectory during testing. This is contradictory because if non-keyframes are only processed with motion-only bundle adjustment (which is less accurate than full bundle adjustment), then the evaluation on the full trajectory would include these lower-quality estimates. The authors should clarify whether they perform full bundle adjustment on all frames during evaluation, or if they truly evaluate the trajectory as constructed with the mixed keyframe/non-keyframe approach described.

comments.title: Unclear baseline comparison in EuRoC results
comments.quote: In the monocular setting, we achieve an average ATE of  $2.2\mathrm{cm}$ , reducing error by  $82\%$  among methods with zero failures, and by  $43\%$  over ORB-SLAM3 when only comparing sequences where ORB-SLAM3 is successful.
comments.explanation: The 82% reduction claim is unclear and potentially misleading. Looking at Table 3, the methods with zero failures appear to be ORB-SLAM3 (0.016-0.138 avg, but with X failures), DSM (0.126 avg), and our method (0.022 avg). If comparing to DSM: (0.126-0.022)/0.126 = 82.5%, which matches. But the text should explicitly state which baseline methods are included in "methods with zero failures" rather than leaving readers to infer this from the table. Additionally, the 2.2cm figure doesn't match the 0.022m average shown in the table - this appears to be a unit conversion error where 0.022m was incorrectly stated as 2.2cm instead of 2.2cm being 0.022m.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence in ETH-3D ranking claim
comments.quote:
In particular, it has the following advantages:

- High Accuracy: We achieve large improvements over prior work across multiple datasets and modalities. On the TartanAir SLAM competition *[54]*, we reduce error by 62% over the best prior result on the monocular track and 60% on the stereo track. We rank 1st on the
comments.explanation: The sentence claiming first place ranking is incomplete. The text cuts off mid-sentence after "We rank 1st on the" and continues to a page break. This appears to be a formatting or copy-paste error that leaves the claim unfinished. The sentence should be completed to specify what competition or leaderboard the first place ranking refers to, likely the ETH-3D RGB-D SLAM leaderboard mentioned in the next paragraph. Rewrite the complete sentence as "We rank 1st on the ETH-3D RGB-D SLAM leaderboard [41]" to match the claim made later in the paragraph.

comments.title: Contradictory method description
comments.quote: In this sense, our approach borrows the best of both approaches: the smoother objective function of indirect approaches with the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches.
comments.explanation: There appears to be a typo in this sentence. The text states the approach borrows "the greater modeling capacity of indirect approaches" twice, which doesn't make sense in context. Based on the preceding discussion, the second "indirect" should likely be "direct" approaches, as the paper just argued that direct methods can model more information like lines and intensity variations.

comments.title: Typo in mathematical notation
comments.quote: For the reminder of the paper, when we refer to depths, note that we are using the inverse depth parameterization.
comments.explanation:
There is a typographical error in the text. The word 'reminder' should be 'remainder'. This appears to be a simple spelling mistake that should be corrected for clarity.

Rewrite "For the reminder of the paper, when we refer to depths, note that we are using the inverse depth parameterization." as "For the remainder of the paper, when we refer to depths, note that we are using the inverse depth parameterization." because this corrects the spelling error.

comments.title: Grammatical error in co-visibility description
comments.quote: An edge $(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$ means image $\mathbf{I}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{I}_{j}$ have overlapping fields of view which shared points.
comments.explanation:
The sentence contains a grammatical error in the phrase "which shared points". This should be "which share points" or "with shared points" to be grammatically correct.

Rewrite "An edge $(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$ means image $\mathbf{I}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{I}_{j}$ have overlapping fields of view which shared points." as "An edge $(i,j)\in\mathcal{E}$ means image $\mathbf{I}_{i}$ and $\mathbf{I}_{j}$ have overlapping fields of view which share points." because this corrects the grammatical error.

comments.title: Typo in feature extraction description
comments.quote: Features are extracted from each new image added to they system.
comments.explanation: There is a grammatical error in the first sentence. 'they' should be 'the'. This appears to be a simple typo that should be corrected for clarity. Rewrite 'Features are extracted from each new image added to they system.' as 'Features are extracted from each new image added to the system.' because 'they' is grammatically incorrect.

comments.title: Ambiguous correlation volume indexing notation
comments.explanation: The notation in equation (1) is ambiguous. The subscript notation $g_{\theta}(I_i)_{u_1 v_1}$ is unclear - it appears to index a feature vector at pixel coordinates $(u_1, v_1)$, but the notation doesn't distinguish between spatial indexing and channel indexing. Since the feature maps are 3D tensors (height × width × channels), the notation should explicitly indicate which dimensions are being indexed. A clearer notation would be $[g_{\theta}(I_i)]_{u_1, v_1, :}$ to indicate the feature vector at spatial location $(u_1, v_1)$ across all channels.

comments.title: Matrix dimension mismatch in Schur complement
comments.quote:
Separating pose and depth variables, the system can be solved efficiently using the Schur complement with the pixelwise damping factor $\lambda$ added to the depth block

\[ \begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{B}&\mathbf{E}\\
\mathbf{E}^{T}&\mathbf{C}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi}\\
\Delta\mathbf{d}\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}\mathbf{v}\\
\mathbf{w}\end{bmatrix}\qquad\qquad\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi}=[\mathbf{B}-\mathbf{E}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\mathbf{E}^{T}]^{-1}(\mathbf{v}-\mathbf{E}\mathbf{C}^{-1}\mathbf{w}) \] (5)
$\Delta\mathbf{d}=\mathbf{C}^{-1}(\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{E}^{T}\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi})$

where $\mathbf{C}$ is diagonal and can be cheaply inverted $\mathbf{C}^{-1}=1/\mathbf{C}$.
comments.explanation:
The dimension mismatch concern is valid but overstated. For $N$ frames with 6-DOF poses, $\Delta\boldsymbol{\xi}$ has dimension $6N \times 1$, making $\mathbf{B}$ a $6N \times 6N$ matrix. For pixelwise depth updates across $H \times W$ images, $\Delta\mathbf{d}$ has dimension $HW \times 1$, making $\mathbf{C}$ an $HW \times HW$ diagonal matrix. The matrix dimensions are consistent: $\mathbf{E}$ is $6N \times HW$. However, the description of $\lambda$ as "pixelwise" is misleading - it's a scalar added to all diagonal elements of $\mathbf{C}$.

Add clarification: "where $\mathbf{C} = \mathbf{C}_{0} + \lambda\mathbf{I}_{HW}$ with $\mathbf{C}_{0}$ being the original depth Hessian block and $\lambda$ applied uniformly to all $HW$ diagonal elements" because this resolves the ambiguity about how the scalar damping factor is applied to the depth block.

comments.title: Missing convergence guarantee for fixed point
comments.quote: Iterative applications of the update operator produce a sequence of poses and depths, with the expectation of converging to a fixed point $\{\mathbf{G}^{(k)}\} \to \mathbf{G}^*$, $\{\mathbf{d}^{(k)}\} \to \mathbf{d}^*$, reflecting the true reconstruction.
comments.explanation:
The paper claims convergence to a fixed point but provides no analysis or conditions under which this convergence occurs. The update operator combines a neural network (ConvGRU) with a Gauss-Newton optimization step, making convergence analysis non-trivial. Without specifying conditions like Lipschitz continuity of the network updates or contraction properties of the combined system, the convergence claim is unsubstantiated.

Add after this sentence: "Convergence is empirically observed in practice under typical SLAM scenarios with reasonable initial estimates, though theoretical guarantees require additional assumptions about the network's Lipschitz constants and the local convexity of the reprojection error landscape" because this acknowledges the limitation while being transparent about the claim's basis.

comments.title: Inconsistent keyframe selection criteria
comments.quote: We compute distance between pairs of frames by computing the average optical flow magnitude and remove redundant frames. If no frame is a good candidate for removal, we remove the oldest keyframe.
comments.explanation: The keyframe removal strategy appears contradictory. The text states that frames are removed based on being "redundant" as measured by optical flow distance, but then says if no frame is a "good candidate for removal", the oldest keyframe is removed instead. This creates ambiguity about the actual selection criteria. If the oldest frame is always removed when no redundant frames exist, then the system isn't truly selecting based on redundancy but rather falling back to a temporal strategy. The authors should clarify whether this is a fallback mechanism or if there's a specific threshold for what constitutes a "good candidate" that isn't mentioned.

comments.title: Unclear gauge freedom handling in frontend
comments.quote: We fix the first two poses to remove gauge freedom but treat all depths as free variables.
comments.explanation: This statement about fixing the first two poses to remove gauge freedom conflicts with the backend description which rebuilds the frame graph and performs global bundle adjustment. If the backend performs global optimization over the entire history, it's unclear whether the first two poses remain fixed during global optimization or if this constraint only applies during frontend local optimization. The gauge freedom handling needs to be clarified - either the first two poses are permanently fixed throughout the system (which would limit global optimization) or they're only fixed during frontend updates (which should be explicitly stated).

comments.title: Inconsistent edge sampling description
comments.quote: We then sample new edges from the distance matrix in order of increasing flow. With each selected edge, we suppress neighboring edges within a distance of 2, where distance is defined as the Chebyshev distance between index pairs $||(i,j)-(k,l)||_{\infty}=\max(|i-k|,|j-l|)$.
comments.explanation: The edge sampling mechanism has a logical inconsistency. The text says edges are sampled "in order of increasing flow" from the distance matrix, but then describes suppressing neighboring edges using Chebyshev distance between index pairs. However, if edges are sampled purely by flow magnitude, the concept of "neighboring edges" in index space doesn't make sense - edges with similar flow values could be anywhere in the matrix. The authors need to clarify whether they're sampling by flow magnitude first, then applying spatial suppression, or if they're using a combined criterion. As written, the algorithm description is ambiguous.

comments.title: Unclear stereo frame handling
comments.quote: For stereo, we use the exact same system described above, with just double the frames, and fix the relative pose between the left and right frames in the DBA layer. Cross camera edges in the graph allow us to leverage stereo information.
comments.explanation: The description of stereo processing is ambiguous and potentially incorrect. Saying "just double the frames" suggests a naive approach that doesn't account for the temporal synchronization between left and right cameras. In stereo systems, left and right frames are captured simultaneously, not sequentially. The text should clarify whether the system processes stereo pairs as synchronized frames or treats them as sequential frames in the graph. Additionally, fixing the relative pose between left and right frames assumes known calibration, but this isn't mentioned as a requirement elsewhere in the paper.

comments.title: Incorrect percentage comparison in TartanAir results
comments.quote: Our method, on the other hand, runs 16x faster and achieves an error  $62\%$  lower on the monocular benchmark and  $60\%$  lower on the stereo benchmark.
comments.explanation: The percentage calculations are incorrect. Looking at Table 2, for monocular: SuperGlue achieves 0.340 and our method achieves 0.129. The reduction is (0.340-0.129)/0.340 = 62.1%, which matches the claim. However for stereo: SuperGlue achieves 0.119 and our method achieves 0.047. The reduction is (0.119-0.047)/0.119 = 60.5%, which also matches. But the text compares to "the top 2 submissions" when the table shows 3 methods. The comparison should be to the best performing method (SuperGlue), not an average of the top 2. Rewrite as "achieves an error 62% lower than SuperGlue on the monocular benchmark and 60% lower on the stereo benchmark."

comments.title: Inconsistent depth units in ETH3D description
